coredata.dc:title: One-step synthesis of solid state luminescent carbon-based silica nanohybrids for imaging of latent fingerprints
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Feng
Li, Hongren
Cui, Tianfang
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials(CNs) with tunable visible emission are biocompatible, environment friendly and most suitable for various biomedical applications. Despite the successes in preparing strongly fluorescent CNs, preserving the luminescence in solid materials is still challenging because of the serious emission quenching of CNs in solid state materials. In this work, fluorescent carbon and silica nanohybrids (SiCNHs) were synthesized via a simple one-step hydrothermal approach by carbonizing sodium citrate and (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane(APTES), and hydrolysis of tetraethyl orthosilicate(TEOS). The resultant SiCNs were characterized through X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), FT-IR, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. The SiCNs exhibited strong fluorescence in both aqueous and solid states. The luminescent solid state SiCNs power were successfully used as a fluorescent labeling material for enhanced imaging of latent fingerprints(LFPs) on single background colour and multi-coloured surfaces substrates in forensic science for individual identification.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanomaterials
Solid state luminescence
Fingerprint detection
Development

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Imaging of latent fingerprints with SiCNs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and (b) DLS particle size distribution of SiCNHs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum, and (b) C1s, (c) N1s and (d) O1s high-resolution XPS spectra of the SiCNHs.

PL spectra (under different excitation wavelengths) of the dilute aqueous solution (a) and solid state powder (b) of the as-prepared SiCNHs. Inset is the fluorescent photo images of the dilute aqueous solution and solid state powder of the SiCNHs under a 365 nm UV lamp.

(a) Fluorescence images of sebaceous latent fingerprints on aluminum foil obtained with SiCNHs powder. (b) Magnified fluorescence images of fingerprints showing specific patterns including termination (1), bifurcation (2), and crossover (3).

Development of latent fingerprints on various substrates: (black (a), red (b), and green (c))marbles, transparent tapes (d), white ceramic tiles (e), black plastic pages (f), stainless steel sheets (g), and painted wood (h). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Latent fingerprints on multi-coloured surfaces labeled by SiCNHs powder: (a) beer bottle foils, (b) red bull drink bottle foils, (c) mangos, and (d) peachs, and then detected by 365 nm UV irradiation images (a’-d’). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Latent fingerprints aged on the surface of glass for various periods of time, stained by SiCNHs powder and finally detected by 365 nm UV light irradiation: (a) 1 day, (b) 1 week, (c) 1 month, and (d) 3 months.

head.ce:title: One-step synthesis of solid state luminescent carbon-based silica nanohybrids for imaging of latent fingerprints

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Normal College, Shenyang University, Shenyang, 110044, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Applied Chemistry, Shenyang University of Chemical Technology, Shenyang, 110142, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials(CNs) with tunable visible emission are biocompatible, environment friendly and most suitable for various biomedical applications. Despite the successes in preparing strongly fluorescent CNs, preserving the luminescence in solid materials is still challenging because of the serious emission quenching of CNs in solid state materials. In this work, fluorescent carbon and silica nanohybrids (SiCNHs) were synthesized via a simple one-step hydrothermal approach by carbonizing sodium citrate and (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane(APTES), and hydrolysis of tetraethyl orthosilicate(TEOS). The resultant SiCNs were characterized through X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), FT-IR, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. The SiCNs exhibited strong fluorescence in both aqueous and solid states. The luminescent solid state SiCNs power were successfully used as a fluorescent labeling material for enhanced imaging of latent fingerprints(LFPs) on single background colour and multi-coloured surfaces substrates in forensic science for individual identification.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials(CNs), owing to their outstanding optical properties, less toxicity and biocompatibility, superior visible-light photocatalytic activity, and cost-effective preparation, have become popular materials in bioimaging [1,2], sensing [3], optoelectronics [4], and photocatalysis [5–7]. To date, many strategies like choosing different precursors and doping with other heteroatoms have been developed for improving the optical properties to expand their application of CNs [8]. Despite the successes in preparing strongly fluorescent CNs, preserving the luminescence in solid materials is still challenging. In past studies, the solid-state luminescent performance of CNs was not satisfactory because of the strong fluorescence quenching that occurs in the dry and aggregated states [9]. Therefore, the massive research efforts focusing on the applications of fluorescent CNs in aqueous environments, such as bioimaging and chemical sensing, and the development of their solid state properties and applications are in high demand.

Fingerprint detection in forensic sciences is an indispensable source of evidence for identification purposes. Fingerprints acts as the most powerful tool for identifying people because the ridge patterns of every print are unique and immutable. The most commonly found fingerprints at crime scenes are typically latent and can only be visualized and identified by certain techniques. Up to now, various methods, such as powder (metallic, magnetic, or fluorescent powder) dusting [10–12], iodine fuming [13] and mass spectrometry [14] techniques, have been studied and applied for detection of latent fingerprints(LFPs). For example, zinc oxide layer can be added around gold nanoparticles to get luminescent fingermarks [15]. Moret et al. reported application of the fluorescent dye incorporated silica nanoparticles to imaging of LFPs [16]. In particular, the use of quantum dots (QDs) as fluorescent labeling marks in LFPs detection has attracted significant research interest in forensic science; this is because of their unique physical and chemical properties such as small particle size, large surface area, good photochemical stability, and high fluorescent intensity. A series of QDs, including CdS, CdSe, and CdTe QDs, have been explored for LFPs development [17–19]. However, the toxicity of these QDs, especially with Cd2+ being classified as a carcinogen, is of increasing concerns. Moreover, these LFPs detection methods are also tedious. Therefore, a novel type of fluorescent nanoparticles with high efficiency and low toxicity is needed for the development of LFPs in the field of forensic chemistry.

Photoluminescent CNs have become popular imaging and sensing materials in the biomedical field, but their use in fluorescence imaging of LFPs has not been well-recognized. Up to now, several methods, including electrochemical [20], hydrothermal [21], and solid-phase synthesis methods [22], have been used for preparation of fluorescent CNs. Among these methods, the hydrothermal method has been widely applied to prepare various materials [23,24], because of the high reactivity of the reactants, easy control of the solution, little harm to the environment, and low energy consumption under hydrothermal conditions [25]. In this research, we used a typical hydrothermal method to chemically synthesize fluorescent carbon and silica nanohybrids (SiCNHs) powder with uniform size and strong fluorescent intensity that can be used to develop LFPs. Although carbon dots-based hybrid powders have been reported for LFPs detection, their fluorescence are too weak to be seen by naked eyes, and the developed fingerprints using the fluorescence microscopy images with point patterns were obtained [26]. For the first time we report the use of solid state SiCNHs as a novel fluorescent labeling agent for completely developing whole latent fingerprints. We successfully used SiCNHs to clearly image LFPs on various nonporous single background colour substrates, including marbles, transparent tape, white ceramic tiles, black plastic pages, stainless steel sheets, painted wood and multi-coloured surfaces materials (drink bottle foils and fresh fruits). LFPs detection using SiCNHs dry powder as fluorescent labeling markers can be well-defined in terms of finger ridge details, resulting in a good definition of enhanced detection.

Sodium citrate, 3-Aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), ethanol were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai China). All the reagents were of analytical grade and were used as received without further purification.

1.0 g sodium citrate was dissolved in 50 mL deionized water in a 100 mL glass beaker, and then 1.0 mL APTES was added into the solution to form a homogeneous solution under magnetic stirring. Then, 1.0 mL TEOS was added dropwise into the solution followed by further stirring for 30 min. After that the mixture was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 4 h in a constant temperature drying oven. After the above reaction, the reactor was cooled down to room temperature naturally. Subsequently, the prepared SiCNs were collected by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 5 min and washed with deionized water twice and with ethanol once, and then dried at 50 °C for 12 h. The solid luminescent SiCNs dry powder was thus obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging of latent fingerprints with SiCNs
ce:section.ce:para: To collect fingerprints from volunteers, fingers from clean hands were gently wiped across the forehead. Then latent fingerprints were pressed on several different surfaces of objects. For the supreme step, single appositions were collected from one donor on selected substrates. For studying aged fingerprints, the fresh fingerprints were left open to air in Petri dishes at room temperature. To detect the latent fingerprints, SiCNHs in the form of dry powder were carefully deposited onto the surfaces of the substrates, followed by a light brushing action to remove the excess powder. Then the prepared samples were illuminated with UV lamp (6W, 365 nm) and the images of the fingerprints were photographed using a Canon EOS 500D digital camera with a Canon 10–55 zoom lens.

The SiCNHs were characterized by XRD on an X'Pert Pro diffractometer (PANalytical Co., Holland) with graphite monochromatized Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm). They were imaged under a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscopy (JEOL Ltd., Japan) using an accelerating voltage of 200 KV. Dynamic laser scattering (DLS) particle size distribution was tested by DLS (Nicomp380/ZLS, particle sizing systems Santa Barbara, CA, USA). Fourier-transform infrared spectra (FTIR) of sample in solid state (KBr matrix) were measured on a Spectrum One spectrometer (Perkin Elmer Co., USA). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was determined by the ESCALAB250 surface analysis system (Thermo VG Co., USA). PL emission spectra of the solution and dried SiCNHs powder were measured on an F-4600 spectrophotometer (Hitachi Co., Japan) under ambient conditions.
The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the SiCNHs (Fig. 1 ) displays a broad peak at about 2θ = 21.3°, revealing the amorphous nature of the carbon structure [27,28].

From the transmission electron microscope (TEM) image (Fig. 2 a) of the synthesized SiCNHs nanoparticles, it can be seen that SiCNHs forms monodisperse quasi-spherical nanoparticles with an average size of about 100 nm. The size distribution was further confirmed by DLS analysis. Fig. 2b shows the size distribution of SiCNHs synthesized in typical process. According to the result, the prepared SiCNHs have a narrow distribution with an average diameter of 102.4 nm.

In order to confirm the coexistence of amorphous carbon and silica, the SiCNHs are characterized by FTIR spectra (Fig. 3 ). Broad absorption bands at 3100−3500 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibration absorption of O-H and N-H. Amide I (C=O) and Amide II(C-N and N–H) bands at 1644 and 1560 cm−1, respectively [29]. Moreover, the IR peaks located at 1070, 792, and 460 cm−1 correspond to the vibration absorption of Si–O (Si–O–Si and O–Si–O), the stretching vibration absorption of –OH of Si–OH group, and the bending vibration absorption of Si–O–Si [30]. Furthermore, the SiCNHs shows a characteristic absorption of stretching vibration bands of C–H at 2942 cm−1. The IR spectra indicates that the hybrids are composed of amorphous carbon and silica [31,32].

The XPS spectrum shown in Fig. 4 a clearly reveals the presence of C1s, N1s, O1s, and Si2p, respectively. As depicted in Fig. 4a, the strong C1s and O1s peaks indicated that the SiCNHs are mainly composed of carbon and oxygen, and the present N1s and Si2p peaks demonstrated that APTES and TEOS participated in the reaction to the SiCNHs. The C1s high resolution XPS spectrum (Fig. 4b) can be decomposed into four peaks at 284.7, 285.6, 286.5, and 288 eV, which are attributed to C=C/C-C, C–N, C-O/C–OH, and C=O, respectively [33]. The N1s peaks at 399.3 and 401 eV shown in Fig. 4c indicate that nitrogen exists mostly in the form of O=C–NH and amino functions (C–N–H), respectively [34]. The results confirmed the successful incorporation of N into the SiCNHs. The O1s peak in Fig. 4d can also be divided into three component peaks at 531.4, 533, and 534.3 eV. The peaks at 531.4 and 533 eV can be assigned to O=C and HO-C/C–O–C bonding, respectively [35], whereas the peak at 534.3 eV indicates Si-O bonding in agreement with the Si2p peak results. The existence of Si–O groups combining FTIR and XRD analyses further suggest the formation of silica and amorphous carbon hybrid nanocomposites.

As seen in Fig. 5 a, the dilute aqueous solution of the SiCNHs exhibits a maximum emission at 443 nm under an excitation of 340 nm. The maximum emission of the SiCNHs shows little change as the excitation wavelength changed from 300 to 400 nm. Furthermore, the aqueous solution of the SiCNHs shows bright blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light (inset in Fig. 5a), suggesting the presence of fluorescent carbon components in SiCNHs.

From Fig. 5b it can be seen that the PL emission spectra for dried SiCNHs powder exhibits a typical excitation-dependent feature, which indicates that the prepared SiCNHs can also emit strong PL in the solid state. The solid state SiCNHs powder emits blue emission under 365 nm UV light (inset in Fig. 5b), which is in agreement with the result obtained from the SiCNHs in aqueous solution. The aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching is a universal problem for carbon-based nanomaterials when they changed from the liquid phase to the solid state. It has been reported that solid powder of carbon dots (CDots) is not photoactive possibly due to extensive clustering and self-quenching effects [36]. The common method of avoiding this undesirable effect is an incorporation of CDots into solid-state matrices, such as poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) [37], inorganic salts [38], and silica [27]. However, simply loading CDots into the gel would not provide efficient restriction of their movement and still suffer the aggregation induced luminescence quenching during the drying process. In this work, the structural properties of APTES-passivated amorphous carbon with the use of citrate and APTES via a hydrothermal process were verified by FT-IR and XPS analyses. Furthermore, the co-hydrolysis of APTES and TEOS should be able to contribute into the formation of cross-linked gel architecture, which effectively prevents the aggregation of amorphous carbon in the resulting SiCNHs powder. As expected, the obtained solid-state SiCNHs powder can maintain stable and strong luminescence under UV light. The remarkable photoluminescent behaviour of the SiCNHs powder shows great potential for a versatile range of technologically important applications. In a proof-of-concept demonstration, we explore here the use of the SiCNHs powder for latent fingerprint detection.

As can be seen from Fig. 6 a, the SiCNHs powder reveals high quality images that show well-resolved ridge patterns that meet the requirements for individual identification for forensic purposes. In fact, the high quality of the fingerprint images obtained using this technique enables visualization of second level bifurcation, crossover, and termination structures (Fig. 6b). As fingerprint minutiae are generally stable and robust to impression conditions, these detailed features identified in the fingerprint form the basis of personal identification. The successful identification of the detailed substructures indicates the high sensitivity and reliability of the SiCNHs for rapid fingerprint development. The obtained results indicate that the prepared solid state luminescent SiCNHs is useful as fingerprint labeling agents due to its strong photoluminescence and photostable properties.

To determine the applicability, the SiCNHs fluorescent powder was employed for developing fingerprints of different people on various substrates. Sebaceous fingerprints on different substrates, including (black, red, and green) marbles, transparent tapes, white ceramic tiles, black plastic pages, stainless steel sheets, and painted wood, with a single background colour were chosen as substrates. Under a 365 nm UV light excitation, the images on these substrates exhibit bright blue fluorescent patterns, in which the details of fingerprint ridges could be clearly recognized, as shown in Fig. 7 . Thus, clear and well-defined whole fingerprint images with high contrast, sufficient quality, and strong fluorescence could be generated with the solid state SiCNHs power. These observations clearly demonstrate that our developing strategy is versatile and can be applied to detect fingerprints on virtually smooth objects.

We then proceeded with the evaluation of the use of SiCNHs powder in the detection of fingerprints printed on multi-coloured surfaces such as various types of drink bottle foils and fresh fruits (Fig. 8 ). The fingerprints deposited in these surfaces showed a poor contrast under bright field illumination (Fig. 8a–d). When excited by 365 nm UV irradiation, the contrast was enhanced markedly by the blue fluorescence (Fig. 8a’-d’). The fingerprints on these multi-coloured surfaces materials could also be clearly detected, similar to the fingerprint development results on the single background colour materials (Fig. 7). Therefore, our SiCNHs fluorescent powder could be used for developing the latent fingerprints with low background interference.

For practical applications aged fingerprints are commonly encountered for individual identification in forensic sciences. During the aging period it is expected that some of the components of a fingermark, such as squalene and water have evaporated or degraded that affect development [39]. To demonstrate the practical usefulness of the SiCNHs-based fingerprint detection method, a series of fingerprints samples had been aged for different periods of time and were stained by SiCNHs-based fluorescent powder. As shown in Fig. 9 , LFPs aged for up to 3 months could still be clearly identified, indicating that not only fresh but also aged LFPs can be visually imaged by this strategy.

In summary, we have demonstrated an one-step hydrothermal process for fabricating amorphous carbon and silica nanohybrids by carbonizing sodium citrate and APTES, and hydrolysis of TEOS. The obtained SiCNHs emit tunable PL in both aqueous and solid states. The solid state luminescent SiCNHs were then successfully used as effective fluorescent labels for the imaging of latent whole fingerprints on various substrates, including single background colour materials (marbles, transparent tape, white ceramic tiles, black plastic pages, stainless steel sheets, and painted wood) and multi-coloured surfaces (drink bottle foils and fresh fruits) substrates. The whole procedure is simple and rapid. The solid state luminescent SiCNHs are promising candidates for potential applications in forensic sciences.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by Liaoning provincial science and Technology Department of the project (No. 20170540653).
